title: Taxpayers to Clean Up Big Illegal Rubbish Dumps
summary: In England, three huge illegal rubbish dumps are going to be cleaned up, and taxpayers will pay for it. These dumps are in Wigan, Sheffield, and Lancashire, and together they have 48,000 tonnes of waste. Normally, the landowner pays to clean up illegal dumps on private land, but this time the government is helping. People living near the dumps are happy because they have problems like bad smells, rats, and flies. One person, Nicha Rowson, had to move her work from home because of the smell. She is glad but thinks it took too long to decide. The government has a plan to stop waste crime and will check the sites before cleaning them. Another big dump in Kidlington is already being cleaned for over £9 million. Some villagers are angry because a super big dump found by the BBC is not being cleaned yet.

background_read: In England, illegal rubbish dumps are places where people throw waste without permission, causing problems like bad smells and pests. Normally, the person who owns the land must clean it up, but sometimes the government steps in to help when the dumps are very big or dangerous. Taxpayers are people who give money to the government, and this money is used for things like cleaning up waste to keep communities safe and healthy.
Article_Structure: WHO: Taxpayers and the government in England
WHAT: Cleaning up three big illegal rubbish dumps with 48,000 tonnes of waste
WHERE: In Wigan, Sheffield, and Lancashire
WHY: To help people living near the dumps who face problems like smells and pests, and as part of a plan to stop waste crime

perspectives.description: Happy about the clean-up but frustrated it took so long and that taxpayers have to pay.
